coredata.dc:title: Photoluminescence of graphene quantum dots enhanced by microwave post-treatment
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Wentao
Li, Xibing
Sun, Xiaoyan
Ding, Xifeng
Feng, Yanjie
Tang, Yinghua
Zhou, Panpan
Wang, Lixi
Zhang, Qitu
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have a great potential in sensors and bioimaging for their lower toxicity, perfect water solubility and bright luminescence. However, considerable efforts still need to devote to enhance the photoluminescence quantum yields (PLQYs) of GQDs. At first, high PLQYs are guaranteed by confined synthesizing GQDs/layered double hydroxides (LDHs) with well-defined structure. Followed by a convenient microwave post-treatment, PLQYs of GQDs are further improved from 21.0% to 34.6%. After microwave post-treatment, the integrity of π-conjugated system has been enhanced and non-radiative processes also have been reduced inside GQDs, which both confirmed to be responsible for the improvement of PL properties. This work may point out a new direction for enhancing the PLQYs of GQDs. Moreover, owing to the stability and well PL performance of GQDs/LDHs, it has a great potential application in the outdoor anti-counterfeiting inks.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Post-treatment
Microwave
Layered double hydroxides
Quantum yields

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD patterns and (b) FTIR spectrum of MgAl-LDHs, ME/LDH, B-GQDs/LDHS and A-GQDs/LDHs.

TEM images of (a) B-GQDs/LDHs, (b) A-GQDs/LDHs, (c) lattice spacing of B-GQDs/LDHs, Insets are FFT pattern and inverse FFT pattern, (d) TEM of B-GQDs/LDHs, Inset is the cross-sectional HRTEM, SEM images of (e) B-GQDs/LDHs and (f) A-GQDs/LDHs, and (g) schematic diagram of GQD/LDHs.

(a) PL emission spectra of B-GQDs/LDOs (λex = 300 nm) and A-GQDs/LDHs (λex = 320 nm), (inset is excitation and emission spectra), Photoluminescence and Excitation (PLE) contour maps of (b) B-GQDs/LDHs and (c) A-GQDs/LDHs, (d) the CIE chromaticity coordinates diagram, (e) and (f) Emission spectra under difference excitation (from 270 nm to 330 nm) and of B-GQDs/LDOs and A-GQDs/LDHs, and TEM images of (g) B-GQDs/LDOs and (h) A-GQDs/LDHs, insets are the size distribution of GQDs.

(a) UV–vis spectra of ME/LDHs, B-GQDs/LDOs and A-GQDs/LDHs, (b) Room-temperature luminescence decay curves of B-GQDs/LDOs and A-GQDs/LDHs, and (c) Representation for the PL mechanism of B-GQDs/LDOs and A-GQDs/LDHs.

(a) A-GQDs/LDHs-PVA anti-counterfeiting ink usage diagram. (b) and (d) night and daytime digital photographs of the wall with the characters of “NANJING TECH UNIVERSITY” under 365 nm UV light and (c), (e) Letters of “NJ TECH” written under water upon excitation with a 365 nm UV lamp off and on.

head.ce:title: Photoluminescence of graphene quantum dots enhanced by microwave post-treatment

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering
Nanjing University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Nanjing University of Science and Technology, Nanjing 211816, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have a great potential in sensors and bioimaging for their lower toxicity, perfect water solubility and bright luminescence. However, considerable efforts still need to devote to enhance the photoluminescence quantum yields (PLQYs) of GQDs. At first, high PLQYs are guaranteed by confined synthesizing GQDs/layered double hydroxides (LDHs) with well-defined structure. Followed by a convenient microwave post-treatment, PLQYs of GQDs are further improved from 21.0% to 34.6%. After microwave post-treatment, the integrity of π-conjugated system has been enhanced and non-radiative processes also have been reduced inside GQDs, which both confirmed to be responsible for the improvement of PL properties. This work may point out a new direction for enhancing the PLQYs of GQDs. Moreover, owing to the stability and well PL performance of GQDs/LDHs, it has a great potential application in the outdoor anti-counterfeiting inks.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays carbon-based nanomaterials, like fullerenes, carbon nanotubes (CNTs), nanodiamind, carbon nanoparticles (CNs) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs), have applied in many fields [1-5]. GQDs, as one kind of carbon materials, have been attracted great attention because of their unique two-dimensional structure, lower biotoxicity, perfect water solubility and chemical inertness [6-8]. These properties make GQDs more useful in optoelectronic devices, sensors and bioimaging [9-11]. In spite of this, the aggregation quenching and non-radiative processes in GQDs are result in low photoluminescence (PL) properties, such as quantum yields (QYs) [8,12-14], which seriously affect the application of GQDs [15,16].

The fluorescence QYs is the number of emitted photons relative to the number of absorbed photons. The larger QYs means the brighter emission [17]. Thus, considerable efforts should be devoted to enhance the PLQYs of GQDs [18-22]. Moreover, clarifying the nature of PL properties improvement inside GQDs is also important [8,23,24]. GQDs, synthesized by top-down and bottom-up method, often exhibit aggregation-induced fluorescence quenching and result in the decrease of PLQYs [13,25]. Layered double hydroxides (LDHs), as typical two-dimensional inorganic materials, which provide a good micro-environment for organic precursors to synthesize GQDs in the interlayer [26-28]. So the aggregation quenching can be well resolved by confined synthesizing in LDHs [29-31]. Producing heteroatom-doped GQDs (such as N-GQDs and N, S-GQDs) by precursor-based synthetic approach could also accomplish the goal of improving PLQYs [32]. But due to the difficulty of aerobic oxidative coupling of amines under conventional synthetic conditions, this method is only adapted to certain synthesis. After successfully obtaining GQDs, PLQYs can be further enhanced by post-treatment [33,34]. On the one hand, GQDs treated with hydrothermal method in ammonia for 10 h, can introduce nitrogen-containing functional groups to the surface of GQDs and change the PLQYs from 2.5% to 16.4% [35,36]. On the other hand, PLQYs can also be improved from 11.7% to 22.9% by stirring GQDs in NaBH4 for 2 h [8,37]. However, with the disadvantages of poisonous chemical and long term experiment, seeking for a more convenient post-treatment to improve the PLQYs is in great request [38]. Therefore in this work, we proposed a fast microwave post-treatment without any chemicals to enhance the PLQYs of GQDs effectively.

Herein, melamine/LDHs (ME/LDHs) were first used as raw materials, and before microwave post-treatment-GQDs (B-GQDs)/ layered double oxides (LDOs) with well-defined structure were prepared by calcination. Followed by a 5 min microwave post-treatment, after microwave post-treatment-GQDs (A-GQDs)/LDHs with higher PLQYs were obtained. Both PL intensity and PLQYs of A-GQDs/LDHs have been greatly improved by microwave post-treatment. UV–vis spectrometer and Transient steady-state fluorescence spectrometer have been used to analyze the absorption spectra and lifetime of GQDs/LDHs. The result confirmed that the change of π-π* transition and non-radiative processes inside the GQDs are responsible for the improvement of PL properties. Considering the stability and bright luminescence of GQDs/LDHs, GQDs/LDHs can be used as a promising anti-counterfeiting material in the fields of outdoor anti-counterfeiting inks [15,39].

MgAl-LDHs were prepared by a co-precipitation method in solutions of constant pH value. 0.045 mol Mg(NO3)2·6H2O (Shanghai, China) and 0.015 mol Al(NO3)3·9H2O (Shanghai, China) dissolved in 60 mL of distilled water (homemade in laboratory) to obtain solution A. 8 g NaOH (Shanghai, China) dissolved in 100 mL distilled water to obtain solution B. Solution A and Solution B were simultaneously added into three-necked flask with pH maintained at 10.5. Then the reaction was kept under nitrogen atmosphere with stirrer rotation speed of 600 r/min for 6 h. After the reaction, the product was centrifugal and washed three times with distilled water, followed by drying at 60 °C.

0.5 g MgAl-LDHs were completely dissolved in distilled water and then mixed with 0.5 g melamines (Shanghai, China) and 20 mL of ethanol (Shanghai, China), then transferred into the teflon-lined stainless steel autoclaves at 200 °C for 24 h. Followed by drying at 60 °C. Then the white product (ME/LDHs) was collected and calcined at 500 °C for 10 h to obtain B-GQDs/LDOs. Microwave post-treatment was then used to improve the performance of B-GQDs/LDOs. In detail, 0.1 g B-GQDs/LDOs was stirred and dispersed in distilled water, and then heated in microwave oven with 650 W power efficiency for 5 min to obtain the sample of A-GQDs/LDHs.

The phase structure of the samples was investigated by powder X-ray diffraction (smartlabTM 9 kW, Rigaku, Japan) with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å). A continuous scanning rate (2θ ranging from 3°to 80°) of 5°(2θ) min−1 was used, with a step size of 0.02°. The morphology was investigated by scanning electron microscope (SEM, Hitachi SU8010, Japan) and transmission electron microscopy (FEI F30) with an acceleration voltage of 220 kV. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy was performed by NICOLET 5700 (Thermo, America). The fluorescence emission, excitation and 3D fluorescence data were performed on a fluorospectrophotometer (Lumina, USA). The ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) diffuse reflectance spectra were measured in the range of 200–800 nm by an UV-3600 spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan), with a slit width of 2.0 nm. Photoluminescence quantum yields (PLQYs) were measured by Edinburgh FLS 980 fluorescence spectrometer equipped with an integrating sphere. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were performed on a Thermo VG ESCALAB 250 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer using Al Kα X-rays as the excitation source. Room-temperature luminescence decay curves was measured by Edinburgh FLS-1000 fluorescence spectrometer (λex = 320 nm).

Morphology and structure of GQDs/LDHs were analyzed by X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), High-resolution TEM (HRTEM), Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and cross-sectional HRTEM. In the case of XRD patterns (Fig. 1 (a)), the XRD pattern of ME/LDHs is mainly containing the characteristic diffraction peaks of melamine (PDF#24–1654) and MgAl-LDHs (PDF#22–0700) before calcination [40]. After calcination, LDHs lose crystal water and are converted into LDO compounds (PDF#45–0946) and the diffraction peaks of Melamine are disappeared by pyrolyzing to B-GQDs [41]. After microwave post-treatment, the diffraction peaks of LDOs are recovered to the peaks of LDHs [42]. This can also be verified by the FTIR spectrum (Fig. 1(b)), which shows the stretching bands of LDHs (1300 ~ 1600 cm−1) [40], and the spectrum of A-GQDs/LDHs shows an absorption band corresponding to the C = C stretching of graphite (1500 cm−1) [43].

After calcination under 500 °C and microwave post-treatment, the HRTEM images shows the lattice fringe of 0.15 nm, which is attributed to the (012) facet of LDHs [44], and the interplanar spacing of 0.21 nm is ascribed to the (100) facet of graphite (Fig. 2 (a), (b), Fig. S1) [45,46]. In Fig. 2c, the fast Fourier transform (FFT) patterns for A-GQDs/LDHs additionally shows that A-GQDs/LDHs had high-crystalline structures with bond length of 0.14 nm, which is in good agreement of their lattice structures [20,47-49]. This result is consistent with the HRTEM results. Fig. 2(a), (b) and (c) are a strong evidence of the formation of GQDs/LDHs. In order to further demonstrate the morphology and structure of GQDs/LDHs, the cross-sectional HRTEM and SEM images of the B-GQDs/LDH platelet are shown in Fig. 2(d), (e) and (f). A hexagonal platelet structure is observed by SEM (Fig. 2(e, f), Fig. S2 and Fig. S3), indicating that B-GQDs/LDOs and A-GQDs/LDHs still retain the morphology and structure of LDHs [15]. The lattice fringes of the cross-sectional HRTEM have a repeating structure, with a smaller spacing of ~ 0.48 nm correspond to the LDH layers and the others with a larger spacing of ~ 0.99 nm are assigned to the GQD layers [15,50]. The combined results from XRD, FTIR, TEM, SEM and HRTEM confirm that the GQD has been formed in the interlayer of LDHs and have the morphology of hexagonal platelet.

Before microwave post-treatment, B-GQDs/LDOs were obtained from ME/LDHs by calcination, and after the microwave post-treatment, B-GQDs/LDOs were changed to A-GQDs/LDHs. UV–vis spectroscopy, PL spectroscopy and steady-state/Lifetime spectrofluorometer were used to investigate the photophysical properties of B-GQDs/LDOs and A-GQDs/LDHs. Fig. 3 a shows the PL emission spectra of B-GQDs/LDOs and A-GQDs/LDHs excited at 300 and 320 nm, respectively, and the same emission maxima at 354 nm. In contrast, A-GQDs/LDHs show a great enhancement of PL intensity after microwave post-treatment. The influence of LDOs in PL property was discussed at Fig. S4(a) and (c), which indicate that the enhancement was come from the GQDs. The difference of photoluminescence excitation (PLE) and emission spectra are shown in the inset. Fig. 3(b-d) are the PLE contour maps and the CIE chromaticity coordinates diagram of B-GQDs/LDOs and A-GQDs/LDHs, which indicate the similarity in emission peaks and blue fluorescence. The PLQYs of B-GQDs/LDOs and A-GQDs/LDHs in Fig. 3(b) were measured using an absolute PLQYs measurement system [51]. After microwave post-treatment, the PLQYs were enhanced from 21.0% to 34.6%. Fluorescence spectra (under different excitation wavelengths), size distribution, UV–vis absorption and the room-temperature luminescence decay curves were used to analyze the property improvement caused by microwave post-treatment. No apparent PL emission peak shift is observed in Fig. 3(e and f) when both B-GQDs/LDOs and A-GQDs/LDHs are excited from 270 to 330 nm, which is different from other reported GQDs [52-54]. Fig. S5 are pictures of samples obtained at different stages under day light and 365 nm excitation. Fig. 3(g-h) show several black dots on the LDH platelets, which are confirmed to be GQDs [55]. By calculating these black dots, B-GQDs/LDOs and A-GQDs/LDHs have a diameter range from 3.0 nm to 21.0 nm. The recent report demonstrating that excitation-independent emission property was usually found in a high uniformity of GQDs in size [49,56,57], in contrast, B-GQDs/LDOs and A-GQDs/LDHs have a wide size distribution in Fig. 3. So, surface/edge states play a more important role in the PL mechanism of B-GQDs/LDOs and A-GQDs/LDHs [58], which can also be confirmed by Fig. S6. In Fig. S6, changes of C1s spectra prove that C = O in B-GQDs are reduced to C-OH after microwave post-treatment, which confirm that the π-conjugate system is more complete after microwave post-treatment. For the UV − vis spectra in Fig. 4 (a), B-GQDs/LDOs and A-GQDs/LDHs exhibited two absorption shoulder bands at 250 and 280 nm, which are both assigned to the π-π* transition of aromatic sp2 domain [59-61]. After microwave post-treatment, the absorption peaks of A-GQDs/LDHs are much stronger compared to the original B-GQDs/LDOs. The PL decay of the B-GQDs/LDOs and A-GQDs/LDHs could be fitted by a multi-exponential function (Fig. 4(b)), the lifetime of A-GQDs/LDHs (54.5 μs) is much longer than that of the B-GQDs/LDOs (45.0 μs). According to τ = (Γ + knr)-1 (Γ is the emissive rate, and k nr is the non radiative decay rate). Because of the stronger absorption peaks of π-π* transition after microwave post-treatment. Thus, the longer lifetime of A-GQDs/LDHs is much likely caused by the low k nr of A-GQDs/LDHs. So, the non-radiative processes have been suppressed after microwave post-treatment (Table S1) [5,58]. Thus, the microwave post-treatment completed the π-conjugated system and reduced the non-radiative processes, which can also be confirmed by C1s XPS spectra in Fig. S6. Therefore, a model for the mechanism is proposed to explain the PL processes of the two GQDs (Fig. 4(c)). The A-GQDs/LDHs have a stronger absorption and less non-radiative routes corresponding to the UV–vis absorption spectra and the PL decay curves. At least, A-GQDs/LDHs have a great improvement in PL intensity and PLQYs after microwave post-treatment.

In order to demonstrate the practical application of luminescence anti-counterfeiting materials, they are often dispersed in polymer matrices to analysis their performance [13,34,49]. Here poly vinyl alcohol (PVA) was chosen for polymer matrices. A-GQDs/LDHs are dispersed in PVA by a mass ratio of 1:1 [62]. The using processes of A-GQDs/LDHs-PVA are shown in Fig. 5 a. The digital photographs of A-GQDs/LDHs-PVA with “NANJING TECH UNIVERSITY” and “NJ TECH” show a good compatibility between A-GQDs/LDHs and PVA in Fig. 5(b-e). As that used under daylight in Fig. 5(d), the mixtures have the same color as the background, demonstrating that the A-GQDs/LDHs-PVA can be used in the field of anti-counterfeiting fields. By printing A-GQDs/LDHs-PVA on the bottom of the watch glass in Fig. 5(c-e), an outstanding anti-counterfeiting effect and waterproof performance are also found. Fig. S7 and S8 shown that A-GQDs/LDHs and A-GQDs/LDHs-PVA have an outstanding PL stability. With these remarkable properties, materials based on A-GQDs/LDHs-PVA have a great potential in the application of outdoor anti-counterfeiting field.

In summary, GQDs/LDHs composite, a well-defined structure of single-layered GQD formed in the interlayer of LDHs, has been prepared by calcining ME/LDHs. After microwave post-treatment, PL properties of GQDs have been greatly enhanced, such as remarkable improvement in PL intensity and PLQYs (from 21.0% to 34.6%). Luminescence behaviors of GQDs have been confirmed that surface/edge states played a major role in PL mechanism of B-GQDs/LDHs and A-GQDs/LDHs. After microwave post-treatment, the reduction of non-radiative processes inside the GQDs and the improvement of the integrity of the π-conjugated system has been identified by absorption spectra and luminescence decay curves. These changes lead to a stronger absorption belonging to the π-π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains and a longer lifetime τ, which can improved the PL intensity and PLQYs. Thus, the microwave post-treatment in this work provides an ingenious direction to promote the PLQYs of GQDs for researchers. In addition, dispersing GQDs/LDHs in polymer matrices (PVA) to gain a kind of anti-counterfeiting inks, which have a great potential in the applications of outdoor anti-counterfeiting field.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Pre-research Foundation of China (6140208010201, 61402080104 and JXXX-2019-058), Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD), the Top-notch Academic Programs Project of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (TAPP, PPZY2015B128), Jiangsu Students innovation and entrepreneurship training program (201810291094Y) and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51202111).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2020.126714.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent applications of carbon nanomaterials in fluorescence biosensing and bioimaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Wen, Y.Q. Xu, H.J. Li, A.P. Lu, S.G. Sun, Recent applications of carbon nanomaterials in fluorescence biosensing and bioimaging, Chem. Commun. 51 (2015) 11346-11358.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Analytical and bioanalytical applications of carbon dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. da Silva, H.M.R. Goncalves, Analytical and bioanalytical applications of carbon dots, Trac-Trends Anal. Chem. 30 (2011) 1327-1336.

Jiang, Ultra-High Quantum Yield of Graphene Quantum Dots: Aromatic-Nitrogen Doping and Photoluminescence Mechanism, Part. Part. Syst. Charact., 32 (2015) 434-440.

Yang, Surface Chemistry Routes to Modulate the Photoluminescence of Graphene Quantum Dots: From Fluorescence Mechanism to Up-Conversion Bioimaging Applications, Adv. Funct. Mater. 22 (2012) 4732-4740.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Graphene quantum dots: emergent nanolights for bioimaging, sensors, catalysis and photovoltaic devices
